Key Causes: 1973 oil crisis, fall of Bretton Woods, steel crisis
Aftermath: Unemployment rate peaked at 9%, slow recovery

Impact on Economies: Global economic recession caused by COVID-19 pandemic
Effect on Industries: Drop in oil prices, collapse of tourism and hospitality industry
Recovery Progress: GDP returning or exceeding pre-pandemic levels by April 2022
Unemployment Statistics: Over 10 million unemployment cases filed in US by October 2020
Global Energy Crisis: Driven by global surge in demand as world exits early recession, strong energy demand in Asia

Impact on Economies: Initial recovery, followed by second downturn in July 1981, strong recovery and expansion through 1990
Effect on Industries: Manufacturing and construction struggled, shift towards neoliberal economic policies
Factors Leading to Recession: Neoliberal policies, contractionary monetary policy, double-digit inflation, energy crisis
trigger: Early 1980s recession in the United States

Factors Leading to Recession: Restrictive monetary policy, loss of consumer and business confidence, 1990 oil price shock, end of the Cold War, savings and loan crisis, slump in office construction
Impact on Western World: Economic downturn, impacts on elections, resignation of leaders, reduction of active companies, high unemployment rates, civil disturbances, growth of discount stores
Canada's Recession: Lasted from April 1990 to April 1992, deeper than US recession, due to higher inflationary pressures, weakening economy in second quarter of 1989, employment growth remained moderate

Affected Regions: European Union, United States, Japan, Russia, others
Main Causes: Slowdown after 1997 Asian financial crisis, dot-com bubble, stock market crash

Contributing Factors: Business-hostile New Deal, antitrust legal threats, labour strikes

Duration: Second quarter of 1953 to first quarter of 1954
Key Figures: James L. Sundquist, Dwight D. Eisenhower, Harry S. Truman
Economic Indicators: Recession cost approximately $56 billion, GDP declined due to government spending and investment
Impact: Described as "relatively mild and brief," caused by events in early 1950s, demand-driven recession of output and employment
Other: Post-Korean War inflation period, restrictive monetary policies, debt-refunding, flexible interest rate policies, increase in pessimism towards the economy, increase in savings

Duration: Recession lasted from mid-1957 to April 1958
Key Figures: Dwight D. Eisenhower, Raymond J. Saulnier, Robert B. Anderson, Lyndon B. Johnson
Economic Indicators: Industrial Production: 142% of 1947-1949 average, Gross National Product: $453 billion annual rate
Impact: Recovery began in May 1958, employment increased by 1 million, unemployment reduced by 1 million, income at new highs
Other: Officially regarded as a moderate recession, government efforts important in recovery

Significant Events: Federal Reserve's monetary policy tightening in 1959, GDP decline of 1.4%
Budget deficits, Vietnam War, Interest Rates
